title: What is the cheapest way to dry clothes?
description: The cheapest way to dry clothes in the UK, compared per load: washing line, airer, dehumidifier, heated airer and tumble dryers, with real 2026 costs.
blurb: Line, heated airer, dehumidifier or tumble dryer? Real per-load drying costs, plus the spin-speed trick that beats them all.
lede: Drying laundry is one of the few household jobs where the cheapest method and the most expensive method differ by a factor of well over a hundred. An outdoor line costs nothing. An old condenser tumble dryer running four times a week costs around £240 a year at current prices. Between those two extremes sit heated airers, dehumidifiers and heat pump dryers, and the right choice depends on your space, your patience and the British weather. This guide compares every common method per load at the current Ofgem price cap, and explains the one habit that cuts the cost of all of them.
short_answer: The cheapest way to dry clothes is outdoors on a line, which costs nothing. Indoors, the cheapest active method is usually a dehumidifier running next to an ordinary airer in a closed room, at roughly 34p a load, with a heated airer or heat pump tumble dryer close behind at about 47p. A standard condenser or vented tumble dryer costs £1 or more per load at 26.11p per kWh. Whatever you use, an extra high-speed spin in the washing machine first costs about a penny and saves far more than that in drying energy.

sections.h2: Every drying method compared, per load
sections.html:
All the figures below assume a typical full load from a washing machine, spun at 1,200 to 1,400 rpm so it starts off holding roughly two litres of water. The job of every drying method is the same: evaporate that water. The differences in cost come down to where the energy comes from and how efficiently the method uses it.

MethodEnergy per loadCost per loadCost per year (4 loads a week)
Outdoor washing line or rotary airer0 kWh£0.00£0.00
Unheated airer in a ventilated room~0 kWh directPennies, but see the radiator section belowVaries
Dehumidifier plus unheated airer (250W, ~5 hours)~1.3 kWh34p£71
Heated airer with cover (230W, ~8 hours)~1.8 kWh47p£98
Heat pump tumble dryer~1.8 kWh47p£98
Vented tumble dryer~3.9 kWh£1.02£212
Condenser tumble dryer (non heat pump)~4.4 kWh£1.15£239

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Energy per load varies with load size, fabric and machine age; treat these as typical mid-range figures.

Two things stand out. First, the gap between a heat pump dryer and an older condenser dryer is around £140 a year at four loads a week, which is why a heat pump dryer often pays for its price premium within a few years in a household that dries by machine. Second, the dehumidifier-and-airer combination quietly beats everything except free outdoor drying, and it solves the damp problem that indoor drying otherwise creates.

sections.h2: The spin cycle: the cheapest drying trick of all
sections.html:
Before any clothes meet air or heat, the washing machine has already done most of the work, and it can do more of it for almost nothing. Spinning water out mechanically is dramatically cheaper than evaporating it. An extra 10 to 15 minute spin uses roughly 0.05 kWh, which is about a penny and a half at current rates. Evaporating the same water in a condenser dryer would cost twenty times that.

In practice this means:

  • Use the highest spin speed your fabrics allow. Moving from 1,000 rpm to 1,400 rpm typically cuts the residual moisture in a load by around a sixth, which knocks a similar share off the drying time and cost of whatever method follows.
  • Run a second spin for towels and jeans. Heavy cotton holds the most water and benefits the most. A penny spent on spinning saves several pence of drying, every load.
  • Do not overload the drum. An overfull machine spins less effectively, so clothes come out wetter and cost more to dry.

This is one of those rare savings that costs nothing to adopt and works with every drying method on this page. It sits naturally alongside the other no-spend habits in our quick wins under a tenner list.

sections.h2: Why drying on radiators is a false economy
sections.html:
Draping wet clothes over radiators feels free, and it is one of the most persistent energy-saving myths that it actually is. The physics says otherwise. Evaporating two litres of water absorbs about 1.4 kWh of heat, and that heat has to come from somewhere. When clothes dry on a radiator, it comes straight out of your central heating. With gas at 7.33p per kWh and a boiler running at typical efficiency, that is roughly 12 to 15p of gas per load, which sounds modest until you add the side effects.

The side effects are the real cost:

  • Blocked heat output. Wet fabric over a radiator stops it heating the room, so the boiler runs longer to hit the thermostat setting and the room feels colder while it does.
  • Moisture into the air. Those two litres of water end up as vapour in your home. In winter, with windows shut, that condenses on cold glass and walls and feeds mould. Treating a mould problem costs far more than any drying saving.
  • Slower heating response. Rooms with covered radiators take noticeably longer to warm, which tempts people to turn the thermostat up.

If you dry indoors without a machine, the better pattern is an airer in a room you can either ventilate well or close off with a dehumidifier running, not fabric on the heat emitters. The same moisture logic applies to bathrooms and kitchens, and it is one of the items on our winter energy checklist precisely because the damage shows up in the coldest months.

sections.h2: Dehumidifier plus airer: the indoor sweet spot
sections.html:
A compressor dehumidifier running next to an ordinary clothes airer in a small closed room is the cheapest active drying method for most UK homes. A typical 250W unit pulling water out of the air for five hours uses about 1.3 kWh, or 34p per load at the current cap rate, and a decent unit in laundry mode will dry a spun load in an afternoon or overnight.

It wins on more than price:

  • It removes the moisture entirely. The water ends up in the tank, not on your windows. That makes it the only indoor method that actively protects against condensation and mould rather than causing them.
  • Its waste heat is useful. A dehumidifier releases the heat it extracts back into the room, plus the heat from its own motor, so the drying room ends up slightly warmer and much drier. Dry air also feels warmer at the same temperature.
  • It is gentler on clothes than tumbling. No lint, no shrinkage, no tangled duvet covers.

The technique matters: use a small room, shut the door, point any airflow across the airer, and empty the tank or use the drain hose. We cover the comparison with simply heating the room in detail in dehumidifier vs heating to dry air, and the short version is that the dehumidifier wins comfortably because moving water takes far less electricity than evaporating it with resistance heat.

A heated airer lands in a similar cost band, around 47p a load for a 230W model run for eight hours, and is a reasonable choice where there is no spare room to close off. Use the fitted cover or drape a clean sheet over it; trapping the warm air roughly halves the drying time and the cost.

sections.h2: If you use a tumble dryer, make it a cheap one to run
sections.html:
Tumble dryers are not all alike, and the type matters more than the brand. A heat pump dryer recycles its heat in a closed loop and uses around 1.8 kWh per full load. An older condenser or vented machine generates fresh heat continuously and uses 4 kWh or more for the same job. At four loads a week, that difference is roughly £140 a year at the current cap rate, which is why a failing old dryer is usually worth replacing with a heat pump model rather than repairing.

Whatever dryer you own, these habits cut its cost:

  • Use the sensor programme, not the timer. Sensor drying stops when the clothes are dry instead of running a fixed period, and avoids both damp loads and wasteful over-drying.
  • Clean the filter every load and the condenser or heat exchanger monthly. A clogged filter forces longer cycles. This is a five-minute job that directly shortens every run.
  • Dry full loads, and similar fabrics together. Half loads use nearly as much energy as full ones, and mixing towels with shirts means the shirts are over-dried by the time the towels finish.
  • Shift it off-peak if your tariff allows. On a time-of-use tariff with a 7p overnight rate, a heat pump dryer load drops to about 13p. Dryers with delay-start timers make this easy, though never run one while you are asleep if the manufacturer advises against unattended use.

Partial outdoor drying also stacks with machine drying: an hour on the line on a breezy day, even a cold one, removes a large share of the water for free, and the dryer only finishes the job.

Free wins when the weather allows: an outdoor line costs nothing and works on any dry, breezy day, including in winter. Indoors, a dehumidifier beside an ordinary airer in a closed room is the cheapest active method at around 34p a load, followed closely by a heated airer or a heat pump tumble dryer at about 47p. Old-style condenser and vented dryers are the expensive outliers at £1 or more per load, around £200 to £240 a year for a typical household.

Three habits improve every method: spin at the highest speed your fabrics allow, never dry on radiators in a closed-up house, and move whatever drying you can outdoors or off-peak. Get those right and laundry drying drops from one of the larger lines on an electricity bill to a rounding error.

faq.q: Is a heated airer cheaper than a tumble dryer?
faq.a: It depends on the dryer. A heated airer at around 230W run for eight hours uses about 1.8 kWh, or 47p a load at 26.11p per kWh, which is roughly half the cost of an old condenser or vented tumble dryer. Against a heat pump dryer the running cost is about the same, but the dryer finishes in under two hours while the airer takes most of a day. Using the airer's cover speeds it up and cuts the cost further.

faq.q: Is it bad to dry clothes indoors without a dehumidifier?
faq.a: It can be in winter. A full load releases around two litres of water into the air as it dries. In a ventilated room or with a window cracked open that is fine, but in a sealed, heated home the moisture condenses on cold surfaces and encourages mould. Either ventilate the drying room, run a dehumidifier alongside the airer, or dry in an unheated spare room with the door shut.

faq.q: Does drying clothes on radiators cost money?
faq.a: Yes. The heat that evaporates the water comes from your central heating, typically 12 to 15p of gas per load, and the covered radiator heats the room less so the boiler runs longer. The bigger cost is the moisture released into the house, which drives condensation and mould in winter. An airer placed near, but not on, a radiator avoids most of the problem.

faq.q: How much does a tumble dryer cost per load in 2026?
faq.a: At the July to September 2026 price cap rate of 26.11p per kWh, a heat pump dryer costs about 47p per full load, a vented dryer about £1.02 and a non heat pump condenser dryer about £1.15. At four loads a week that is roughly £98, £212 and £239 a year respectively.

faq.q: What is the cheapest time of day to dry clothes?
faq.a: On a standard tariff the price is the same all day, so timing only matters for outdoor drying, where late morning to mid afternoon gives the best wind and sun. On a time-of-use tariff with a cheap overnight window, running a tumble dryer or heated airer off-peak can cut the electricity cost by two thirds or more, for example about 13p for a heat pump dryer load at a 7p overnight rate.
